title: German Numbers for Tourists - Quick Summary
items: **The big rule: the small number comes first.** German says the units before the tens, so 21 is einundzwanzig, literally "one-and-twenty," and 67 is siebenundsechzig, "seven-and-sixty." Read the word right to left and it matches English. This one flip causes most tourist mix-ups at a till or on a platform.

**A price uses a comma, not a dot.** "3,50 €" means three euros fifty, and you will usually hear it as "drei fünfzig," two numbers with no "and" in between. A dot is the thousands separator, so 1.000 means one thousand, not one point zero.

**Three spelling traps to expect.** sechs loses its s in sechzehn (16) and sechzig (60); sieben loses its -en in siebzehn (17) and siebzig (70); and 30 is dreißig, ending in -ßig, not -zig like the other tens.

**Platforms and lines are plain numbers.** A platform is a Gleis, so "Gleis sechzehn" is track 16 at Berlin Hauptbahnhof. U-Bahn and S-Bahn lines are just the digit: U8 is "U acht," S7 is "S sieben." On platforms you will often hear zwo instead of zwei so 2 and 3 do not blur.

**Cents and the Pfand.** One hundred cents make a euro. The Pfand deposit on a can or single-use bottle is 25 cent, said "fünfundzwanzig Cent," and you get it back at the return machine. In 2026 a single AB transport ticket is 4,00 €, said "vier Euro."
